LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS PILOT AI LABS, INC., JONATHAN SU, AND ROBERT ENGLISH'S MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION. A R G U E D. THE COURT CONFIRMS HIS TENTATIVE RULING, AND ASK COUNSEL GO BACK TO THE DRAWING BOARD ON THE ORDER, REWRITE THE SECOND TO THE LAST SENTENCE AND ADD AN ADDITIONAL SENTENCE OR TWO WITH REGARD TO COSTS. THE MOTION IS GRANTED IN LARGE PART. SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR THE COURT'S COMPLETE RULING. COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANT IS TO SUBMIT A FROM OF ORDER THAT COMPLIES WITH CRC 3.1312 PLAINTIFF'S COUNSEL (AS FULLY REPORTED IN THE REPORTER TRANSCRIPTS) IF THERE'S A DISAGREEMENT, SEND A LETTER TO THE COURT AS TO THE PARTIES' DISAGREEMENT. JUDGE: HAROLD E. KAHN; CLERK: W. TRUPEK; COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO CSR# 8600 EMAIL: MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-08-2019 Continued To May-29-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Feb-13-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Arena Hoffman LLP, Arena Hoffmn LLP,Telephone 415-433-1414; appearing for Plaintiff;
Randal Arena, Esq., Arena Hoffmn, LLP Telephone 415-433-1414; appearing for Plaintiff;
Alan Matkins, Esq., Telephone 415-273-7444, appearing for Defendant Piloy AI. et al;

Ruling - A R G U E D. The Court confirms his tentative ruling, and ask counsel go back to the drawing board on the order, rewrite the second to the last sentence and add an additional sentence or two with regard to costs. (as reported in the Reporter's Notes)

Defendants Pilot AI Labs, Inc., Jonathan Su, and Robert English's motion to compel arbitration is granted in large part. This case is stayed pending the conclusion of arbitration proceedings on all of the claims filed by plaintiff Rachel Moore other than her PAGA claim. Ms. Moore's PAGA claim is not subject to arbitration per the California Supreme Court's decision in Iskanian. Considered singly or collectively, none of the litigation conduct of the moving defendants constitutes a waiver of their right to arbitrate the claims of plaintiff Rachel Moore nor has Ms. Moore suffered prejudice from any of that conduct so that moving defendants have forfeited their contractual right to arbitration set forth in the March 24, 2017 offer of employment letter. A party's failure to allege an affirmative defense of arbitration or timely file an arbitration petition does not constitute either waiver or prejudice per the California Supreme Court's decision in St. Agnes Medical Center and case law since that decision. Moving defendants' propounding of discovery also does not constitute waiver or prejudice per St. Agnes and subsequent case law at least where, as here, there has been no showing that moving defendants gained information about Ms. Moore's case that they could not have learned about through arbitration. Moving defendants have not hid their desire to have Ms. Moore's claims arbitrated. What was said in Khalatian v. Prime Time Shuttle, Inc. (2015) 237 Cal. App. 4th 657, 663 has equal, if not greater, applicability to this case: "In Iskanian, our Supreme Court rejected the argument that a plaintiff's efforts over three years, including considerable effort and expense, resulted in waiver. Participating in litigation itself does not result in waiver. Causing the other party to incur expenses as part of litigation also does not result in waiver. (Here, there was no evidence that defendants stretched out the litigation process, gained information about plaintiff's case they could not have learned in an arbitration, or waited until the eve of trial to move to compel arbitration. Plaintiff identifies no information revealed during discovery that would not have been revealed in an arbitration...Because plaintiff demonstrated no prejudice from defendants' delay in moving to compel arbitration, the [trial] court erred in finding waiver." (Citations omitted). Ms. Moore has also not shown that there is anything substantively unconscionable about the parties' arbitration agreement. Absent substantive unconscionability, the parties' arbitration agreement is not unenforceable as unconscionable, even assuming there is procedural unconscionability. The carve-out for equitable relief is not substantively unconscionable per the reasoning of Baltazar v. Forever 21, Inc. (2016) 62 Cal.4th 1237 and Farrar v. Direct Commerce, Inc. (2017) 9 Cal.App.5th 1257. Contrary to Ms. Moore's arguments, the parties' agreement does not require Ms. Moore to pay more than she is required to pay per Armendariz nor does that agreement unreasonably limit her discovery rights. Counsel for defendant is to submit a from of order that complies with CRC 3.1312 plaintiff's counsel (as fully reported in the reporter transcripts) If there's a disagreement, send a letter to the Court as to the parties' disagreement. Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600 email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/HEK)

Notice Of Joinder In Trinnet Group, Inc.S And Trinet Hr Iii-A, Inc.S Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Dec-17-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Ronald D. Arena, Esq., Arena Hoffman, Telephone 415-433-1414, appearingfor Plaintiff;
Satia Famili, Esq., Arena Hoffman, Telephone 415-433-1414, appearing for Plaintiff;
Kevin L. Quan, Esq., Allen Matkins, Telpehone 415-273-7444, appearing for Defendant Pilot AI;
Amy Wintersheimer Findley, Esq., Allen Matkins, Telpehone 415-273-7444, appearing for Defendant, Pilot AI;

Ruling - ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants Pilot AI Labs, Inc., Jonathan Su, and Robert English's joinder in Defendants Trinet Group, Inc. and Trinet HR III-A, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration is denied. The Dispute Resolution Protocol in TriNet's terms and conditions requires Pilot AI to seek to compel arbitration under its own employment agreement with Plaintiff Rachel Moore. The DRP indicates that separate agreements between its customers and its customer's employees (such as the employment agreement between Pilot AI and Ms. Moore) control disputes to the extent such agreements are inconsistent with the DRP. The employment agreement's arbitration clause contains materially different terms. Arbitration is specifically sited in Santa Clara County and the arbitration is governed by the California Arbitration Act rather than the Federal Arbitration Act. Moreover, the employment agreement's integration clause suggests that other agreements like the DRP should not apply or conflict with the exclusive agreement between Pilot AI and Ms. Moore. Therefore, the employment agreement controls Ms. Moore's claims against Pilot AI, Mr. Su, and Mr. English, not the DRP. The Pilot AI defendants are only third-party beneficiaries of the DRP to the extent their own arbitration clause with Ms. Moore does not conflict with the DRP. Similarly, equitable estoppel arguments and California's policy favoring arbitration cannot overcome the clear language of these two contracts. If the Pilot AI defendants wish to bring Ms. Moore's claims against them into arbitration, they must seek to do so through their own petition to compel arbitration under their own arbitration clause, not by way of joinder to motion of the TriNet entities. Defendants' evidentiary objections are overruled. Prevailing party to prepare a proposed order.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/HEK)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-13-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Ronald Arena, Esq. and Michael Moore, Esq. 415-433-1414 for Plaintiff.
Krista Cabrera, Esq. 858-847-6718 of Foley & Lardner LLP for Defendant NEA.
Kevin Quan, Esq. 415-837-1515 of Allen Matkins for Defendant Pilot AI.
Christopher Boman, Esq. 949-851-2424 of Fisher & phillips LLP for Defendant Trinet through Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT NEW ENTERPRISE ASSOCIATES 15, L.P.'S (ERRONEOUSLY NAMED AS NEW ENTERPRISE ASSOCIATES)DEMURRER.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.Defendant New Enterprise Associates 15, L.P.'s demurrer to all twelve causes of action in the complaint filed by plaintiff Rachel Moore is sustained without leave to amend as to all twelve causes of action. Ms. Moore has not alleged, nor does it appear that she can allege, sufficient ultimate facts to show that the alleged misconduct of Rick Yang is attributable to NEA. The factual allegations of the complaint (as opposed to the conclusions of law therein) show that at all times Mr. Yang participated in any of the conduct for which Ms. Moore seeks to hold NEA liable, Mr. Yang was acting in his capacity as a board member of defendant Pilot AI, Inc., not as an agent of NEA. Because NEA possesses no legal right to control Pilot AI's board member activities, as a matter of law Mr. Yang was not acting as NEA's agent while he oversaw the investigation into Ms. Moore's harassment complaint. Furthermore, NEA is not liable based on respondeat superior because sexual harassment is not an "inevitable risk" flowing from NEA's venture capital enterprise. Regarding FEHA aiding and abetting, Gibson-Jones v. Berkel & Company Contractors (N.D. Cal. 2008) 2008 WL 782568 is distinguishable because that case only stands for the proposition that an aiding and abetting theory may apply to an investigator who allegedly engaged in abuses. Gibson-Jones does not state or suggest that aiding and abetting liability may be extended to the employer of a board member who oversaw such an investigation. The joint employer theory is inapplicable because, even with added information about NEA's involvement in recruitment and some trainings, Ms. Moore cannot allege sufficient facts that NEA had control over her day-to-day employment activities. The integrated enterprise theory is likewise inapplicable because that theory only applies to a parent company and subsidiary, not to an investor and a portfolio company. Finally, because FEHA ratification can only be done by an employer in response to actions of its employees and because NEA is not the employer of defendants Jonathan Su and Robert English, NEA cannot have ratified their alleged misconduct. Since there is no indication that Ms. Moore can amend her complaint to allege a viable claim against NEA, leave to amend is denied.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/HK)
